core-env.title: Environment & Ozone Depletion
core-env.lesson: Imagine a single chlorine atom drifting into the stratosphere. It seems tiny and harmless, right? But here is why it matters: that one atom will survive up there for 120 years, and during that time it can destroy up to 100,000 ozone molecules. Stratospheric ozone (O3) is a molecule made of 3 oxygen atoms that forms a protective shield blocking harmful ultraviolet radiation from the sun. When CFC or HCFC refrigerants release chlorine into the stratosphere, a destructive cycle begins. The chlorine atom steals one oxygen from O3, forming chlorine monoxide (ClO) and leaving O2 behind. Then the ClO collides with another ozone molecule, releases the oxygen, and frees the chlorine to attack again. It is a chain reaction that never stops. CFCs and HCFCs cannot dissolve in water, so they cannot be rained out of the atmosphere. NASA confirmed the rise in stratospheric chlorine matches CFC and HCFC emissions, not volcanic activity. Volcanoes contribute only small quantities. The damage from ozone depletion is global, not local. The most serious human health effect is increased skin cancer rates, followed by cataracts, decreased crop yields, and damage to marine organisms.
keyNumbers: 100,000 ozone molecules destroyed per chlorine atom
core-env.memoryTrick: 1 Cl atom = 120 years = 100K ozone kills. Think: one tiny villain with a 120-year rampage.
core-env.examWarning: Do not confuse ODP with GWP. ODP measures ozone destruction (chlorine). GWP measures global warming contribution. They are completely separate scales.

core-caa.title: Clean Air Act & EPA Authority
core-caa.lesson: Picture this: a technician vents refrigerant into the air, thinking nobody will notice. That single act could cost them $44,539 per day, per violation, as of 2017. That is not a typo -- per DAY, per violation. Section 608 of the Clean Air Act sets the rules for refrigerant handling in the HVACR industry. It requires certification for anyone performing any activity that violates the refrigerant circuit. This means if you are opening a system, removing refrigerant, or replacing a component containing refrigerant, you need your Section 608 card. You must carry it when performing regulated activities. Lost it? Contact your certifying organization, not the EPA. Here is what you do NOT need certification for: servicing external electric circuits or sections not part of the refrigerant circuit. The Clean Air Act makes it illegal to knowingly release CFC, HCFC, or HFC refrigerants during service, maintenance, repair, or disposal. Violations can result in fines, loss of certification, and criminal prosecution. One critical detail: state and local governments can set stricter requirements than federal EPA regulations. You must also comply with any future regulation changes after becoming certified.
core-caa.memoryTrick: 608 = the gate to refrigerant work. $44,539 = the daily price of breaking the rules. Think: '44-5 = 44 thousand, 5 hundred' per day.
core-caa.examWarning: The fine is PER DAY PER VIOLATION, not a one-time penalty. Also, you do NOT need 608 certification for electrical circuit work outside the refrigerant circuit.

core-regs.title: Regulations & Compliance
core-regs.lesson: Think of the Montreal Protocol as a global handshake between nations agreeing to stop destroying the ozone layer. The United States is part of this international treaty addressing ozone-depleting substances and their phase-out schedules. The EPA created the SNAP Program (Significant New Alternatives Policy) to identify refrigerants with lower overall risks to human health and the environment as substitutes for ozone-depleting substances. Here is the critical timeline: production and imports of newly manufactured R-22 and HCFC-142b were phased out in 2020. After the complete HCFC phase-out in 2030, supplies for servicing equipment will come only from recovered and reclaimed sources. But here is what catches people: it is NOT a violation to continue servicing systems that use CFCs or HCFCs after their phase-out. You can still legally service an R-22 system using existing supplies. What you CANNOT do is top off an R-22 charge with a different refrigerant like R-410A. Mixing refrigerants can void warranties, ruin the refrigerant, damage equipment, and create safety hazards. Also remember: EPA does not approve any substitute refrigerants as direct drop-in replacements. There is no such thing as a drop-in substitute for R-22 or any other refrigerant.
core-regs.memoryTrick: 20-30 timeline: R-22 production stops at 2020, all HCFCs gone by 2030. After 2030 = recovery and reclaim only. No drop-ins ever.
core-regs.examWarning: You CAN still service R-22 systems after the phase-out. You CANNOT mix R-22 with R-410A or any other refrigerant. There is NO drop-in replacement.

core-sub.title: Refrigerant Substances & Substitutes
core-sub.lesson: Imagine a family tree of refrigerants where each branch has different chemistry and environmental impact. Understanding this family is essential for the exam. CFCs (like R-11, R-12) contain chlorine, fluorine, and carbon. They have the highest ODP (ozone depletion potential of 1, the baseline) and are phased out. HCFCs (like R-22, R-123) added hydrogen to the mix, making them less harmful to ozone, with ODP between 0 and 1. R-22 has ODP of 0.055. HFCs (like R-134a, R-410A) contain hydrogen, fluorine, and carbon with single bonds. Zero ODP because no chlorine, but high GWP. R-410A has GWP of 2090. HFOs (like R-1234yf) also have hydrogen, fluorine, and carbon but with at least one double bond between carbon atoms. That double bond is the key difference from HFCs. Zero ODP and very low GWP. R-1234yf has GWP of only 4. Hydrocarbons (like R-290 propane, R-600a isobutane) are just hydrogen and carbon. Zero ODP and GWP under 10, but they are highly flammable (A3). CO2 (R-744) has GWP of 1 (the baseline) and ammonia (R-717) has GWP of 0. Both are exempt from venting prohibition.
core-sub.memoryTrick: Chlorine = ozone killer. No chlorine = zero ODP. Family order by ODP harm: CFC > HCFC > HFC/HFO/HC (zero). GWP trick: HFCs have zero ODP but HIGH GWP -- the sneaky ones.
core-sub.examWarning: Do not assume low ODP means low GWP. HFCs have zero ODP but GWP in the thousands. Also, the double bond is what separates HFOs from HFCs.

core-ref.title: Refrigerant Properties & Tools
core-ref.lesson: Imagine you are at a job site and someone hands you a cylinder of refrigerant. How do you know what pressure to expect? The EPA defines four pressure categories, all measured at 104 degrees F condensing temperature. Low-pressure: 30 psig or lower (R-11, R-123, R-245fa, R-1233zd). Medium-pressure: between 30 and 155 psig (R-12, R-134a, R-600a, R-1234yf). High-pressure: between 155 and 340 psig (R-22, R-410A, R-404A, R-407C). Very high-pressure: over 340 psig (R-744/CO2, R-170/ethane). Now for blends: zeotropic mixtures like R-407C have components with different boiling points, creating temperature glide that can range up to 12 degrees or more. These use bubble (liquid) and dew (vapor) points on pressure-temperature charts. Pure and azeotropic refrigerants like R-22, R-134a, and R-410A use single saturation values. Key PT values to memorize: R-134a at 80F equals approximately 87 psig. R-410A at 80F equals 238 psig. R-22 at 80F equals 144 psig. When charging zeotropic blends, bubble point values are for subcooling, dew point values are for superheat. Always charge blends as liquid to prevent fractionation.
core-ref.memoryTrick: Pressure categories at 104F: 30/155/340 are the dividing lines. PT trio at 80F: 134a=87, 22=144, 410A=238. Bubble=liquid=subcooling. Dew=vapor=superheat.
core-ref.examWarning: All pressure categories reference 104 degrees F, not room temperature. Do not use single PT values for zeotropic blends like R-407C -- they need bubble and dew points.

core-3rs.title: Recovery, Recycling & Reclamation
core-3rs.lesson: Think of these three R's as levels of refrigerant cleaning, from basic to hospital-grade. Recovery simply means removing refrigerant from a system and storing it in an approved container. The refrigerant may go back into the same system, another system with the same ownership, or to a reclaimer. Recycling goes further: the refrigerant is extracted and cleaned using oil separation and filter passes for reuse in the same owner's equipment. Recycling equipment must meet AHRI Standard 740. Reclamation is the gold standard: refrigerant is processed to virgin-product specifications through chemical analysis at a processing company. Reclaimed refrigerant must meet AHRI Standard 700 before it can be sold. Here is the critical rule: reclaimed refrigerant is the ONLY recovered refrigerant that can change ownership and be sold. Used refrigerant cannot change ownership without reclamation. All recovery and recycling equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993 must be certified by an approved laboratory and carry an EPA-approved certification label. You need a separate recovery cylinder for each refrigerant type, even if two refrigerants are both HFCs.
keyNumbers: Separate cylinder for each refrigerant type
core-3rs.memoryTrick: 700 = reclaim = can be SOLD (7 looks like a dollar sign). 740 = equipment testing. Only reclaimed refrigerant changes hands. Nov 15, 1993 = equipment birthday.
core-3rs.examWarning: Do not confuse AHRI 700 (reclaimed refrigerant quality) with AHRI 740 (equipment certification). Selling recovered but non-reclaimed refrigerant is illegal.

core-rec.title: Recovery Equipment & Procedures
core-rec.lesson: Imagine two different approaches to moving refrigerant out of a system. Self-contained (active) recovery devices have their own compressor or pump to draw refrigerant out. System-dependent (passive) recovery relies on the appliance's own compressor or refrigerant pressure to push it into a container. Active equipment is more powerful, but passive can work for smaller jobs. Practical tips that save time and prevent problems: use short, large-diameter hoses to avoid pressure drop and speed up recovery. Long, small hoses cause excessive pressure drop and slow everything down. Cool the recovery cylinder and heat the system to increase the pressure difference and speed things up. After liquid transfer, guard against trapping liquid in service hoses between closed valves. Hermetic compressor motors in recovery machines rely on refrigerant flow for cooling. Never energize them under deep vacuum or they can overheat and suffer winding damage. Before recovery, always determine the total charge and know the refrigerant type. Use separate cylinders for each type -- even two different HFCs like R-134a and R-410A need separate cylinders. Mixing in containers may make refrigerant impossible to reclaim.
keyNumbers: Short + large diameter hoses = faster recovery
Never run recovery compressor in deep vacuum
core-rec.memoryTrick: Active = has its own engine. Passive = hitchhikes on the system. SHORT FAT hoses = FAST recovery. COOL the tank, HEAT the system.
core-rec.examWarning: Even if two refrigerants are both HFCs, they need separate cylinders. Mixing refrigerants in containers may make them impossible to reclaim with added disposal costs.

core-evac.title: Evacuation Procedures & Vacuum
core-evac.lesson: Picture water droplets hiding inside a refrigeration system. If they stay, they will form hydrochloric and hydrofluoric acids during operation, eating your system from the inside. Evacuation is how you remove that water and water vapor. Most manufacturers require evacuation to 500 microns or lower, measured with a micron gauge. Here is the technique: connect vacuum lines equal to or larger than the pump intake. Keep piping to the vacuum pump as short and large as possible. Place your vacuum gauge as far from the pump as possible for accurate readings. When measuring the final vacuum, isolate the system and turn the pump OFF. You cannot over-evacuate a system. Once you reach 500 microns, watch what happens. If pressure rises, stops for a few minutes, then rises again, you have moisture still in the system. If pressure steadily rises without stopping, you have a leak. For systems with heavy moisture, use triple evacuation: evacuate, break vacuum with dry nitrogen, evacuate again, break again with nitrogen, then final evacuation. The nitrogen prevents water from freezing, which can happen with large vacuum pumps. Heating the system during evacuation decreases dehydration time.
core-evac.memoryTrick: 500 microns is the magic number. Gauge FAR from pump, pump OFF to read. Rise-stop-rise = moisture (water evaporates in batches). Steady rise = leak (continuous).
core-evac.examWarning: You CANNOT over-evacuate a system. Always measure final vacuum with the pump turned OFF and system isolated. Placing the gauge near the pump gives false readings.

core-safe.title: Safety & Refrigerant Handling
core-safe.lesson: Here is a sobering fact: in most fatal refrigerant accidents, oxygen deprivation is the major cause of death. Refrigerants are heavier than air and sink, displacing the oxygen you need to breathe. Always wear safety glasses and protective gloves when handling refrigerants. Never expose refrigerants to open flames or glowing hot metal surfaces. At high temperatures, refrigerants decompose and form dangerous acids: hydrochloric acid if the refrigerant contains chlorine, hydrofluoric acid if it contains fluorine. If oxygen is also present, decomposition can form carbon monoxide, CO2, and phosgene gas. Heating a refrigerant cylinder can cause an explosion. For flammable refrigerants like hydrocarbons and some HFOs, recovery machines must be grounded. Never apply open flame or live steam to a flammable cylinder. Never use oxygen to purge or pressurize any system, as mixed with refrigerants or compressor oil it can cause an explosion. When pressurizing with nitrogen, always charge through a pressure regulator with a relief valve downstream. Multiple relief valves must be installed in parallel only, never in series. If a large refrigerant release occurs in an enclosed area, use a self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) or leave the area immediately.
keyNumbers: Relief valves = PARALLEL only, never series
core-safe.memoryTrick: Refrigerants SINK (heavier than air) and SUFFOCATE (displace O2). NEVER: flame + refrigerant, oxygen + system, relief valves in series. ALWAYS: nitrogen + regulator, parallel relief valves.
core-safe.examWarning: Even A1 (safest) refrigerants can cause asphyxiation in high concentrations. Never confuse nitrogen (safe for pressurizing) with oxygen (explosion risk with refrigerants).

core-ship.title: Shipping, Disposal & Transport
core-ship.lesson: Imagine loading recovery cylinders into a truck for transport. What rules keep everyone safe? Recovery cylinders must be free of rust and damage, labeled, secured, and filled to no more than 80% of capacity by weight. Approved recovery cylinders have yellow tops and gray bodies. Reusable high-pressure containers (above 15 psig) must be hydrostatically tested every 5 years. Disposable cylinders may NEVER be used for recovery. When emptying disposable cylinders, recover vapor to 0 psig before scrapping. For transport, a DOT classification tag and label must be attached to cylinders containing used refrigerant. Cylinders must be positioned upright when shipped. DOT regulations require recording the number of cylinders of each gas on shipping papers for hazard class 2.2 (nonflammable compressed gas). The label on recovery cylinders prevents accidental mixing, helps recyclers identify contents, and enables proper handling. For disposal of appliances, the last person in the disposal chain is responsible for ensuring proper refrigerant recovery. When disposing of appliances with 5 to 50 pounds of refrigerant, EPA does not require recording model and serial numbers, but you must keep records of type, quantity, date, and location for 3 years.
keyNumbers: Yellow top + gray body = recovery cylinder
core-ship.memoryTrick: Yellow-gray = recovery. 80% max fill. 5-year hydrostatic test. Ship UPRIGHT with DOT tag. Last person in chain = responsible. 3 years of records.
core-ship.examWarning: Disposable cylinders can NEVER be used for recovery. The fill level is controlled by mechanical float, electronic shut-off, or weight scale -- NOT by the pitch of the recovery device.

t1-rec.title: Small Appliance Recovery
t1-rec.lesson: Imagine you are called to service a household refrigerator. This is Type I territory: small appliances that are factory-charged, hermetically sealed, and contain 5 pounds or less of refrigerant. Split systems are NOT small appliances, regardless of charge size. For recovery, you can use either self-contained (active) or passive equipment. A domestic refrigerator is a textbook example where passive recovery is permissible. But passive recovery is limited to appliances with a maximum charge of 15 pounds. Self-contained devices must recover 90% of the refrigerant when the compressor is working, and 80% when the compressor is inoperative. When the compressor does not run, access both the high and low side of the system for better recovery speed. Heat the compressor with a heat gun and strike it with a rubber mallet to free refrigerant trapped under the oil in the crankcase. For frost-free refrigerators, run the defrost heater to help with complete recovery. If an appliance with an operating compressor has a completely restricted capillary tube, you only need one access valve on the high side. If the system reads 0 psig after installing a piercing valve, recovery cannot begin.
t1-rec.memoryTrick: Type I = 5 lbs or less, factory sealed. 90/80 rule: 90% with compressor, 80% without. Passive maxes at 15 lbs. Zero psig = zero recovery.
t1-rec.examWarning: Split systems are NEVER Type I, even if they contain less than 5 lbs. MVAC-like systems also do not qualify for Type I certification.

t1-tech.title: Type I Techniques & Sealed Systems
t1-tech.lesson: Think of a small appliance as a sealed box that left the factory fully charged. The service aperture or process stub is a straight piece of tubing that you enter using a piercing access valve. Before anything else, always know the refrigerant type to prevent mixing in recovery cylinders. After installing any access fitting, leak test it before proceeding with recovery. Here is an important detail: solderless piercing valves tend to leak over time and should NOT remain on the system after you finish the repair. Remove them when done. When recovering from an appliance with a non-operating compressor, use both high and low-side access for better speed. Passive recovery can capture refrigerant into non-pressurized containers. A vacuum pump can be used to evacuate a recovery cylinder but CANNOT be used in combination with a pressurized recovery cylinder for recovery. When filling charging cylinders with regulated refrigerant, any refrigerant vented off the top must be recovered. R-744 (CO2) generally does not need to be recovered. If you smell a pungent odor during recovery, suspect compressor burnout. Watch for oil contamination and flush the system if contaminants are present.
keyNumbers: Piercing valves = temporary, remove after service
R-744 generally does not need recovery
t1-tech.memoryTrick: Pierce, test, recover, REMOVE the valve. Two sides for dead compressor. Pungent smell = burnout alarm. CO2 gets a free pass on recovery.
t1-tech.examWarning: Solderless piercing valves must be removed after service. A vacuum pump can evacuate a recovery cylinder but CANNOT be used with a pressurized cylinder for recovery.

t1-safe.title: Type I Safety & Flammable Refrigerants
t1-safe.lesson: Imagine opening a household refrigerator that uses R-600a (isobutane). It is a hydrocarbon, classified A3, meaning highly flammable. Safe work practices start with wearing safety eyewear and butyl-lined gloves when connecting and disconnecting hoses. R-600a is approved for new household refrigerators, freezers, and combination units, but hydrocarbon refrigerants are NOT approved for retrofit into existing household refrigerators designed for other refrigerants. New SNAP regulations also approve R-290 and R-450A for newly manufactured household units. Appliances with HC refrigerants require permanent safety markings on or near evaporators, exposed piping, and other accessible components. A red color marking is required on all process tubes carrying flammable refrigerant where a technician might cut, extending a minimum of 1 inch in both directions. Flammable HC and HFO refrigerants do NOT contain odorants. Unlike natural gas, you cannot smell a leak. Refrigerants are heavier than air and displace oxygen. A large leak in an enclosed area can cause suffocation. Without SCBA, vacate and ventilate naturally. At high temperatures, CFC and HCFC refrigerants decompose into phosgene gas, hydrochloric acid, and hydrofluoric acid.
keyNumbers: R-600a = A3, approved for NEW household units only
t1-safe.memoryTrick: HCs for NEW appliances only, never retrofit. Red marks = 1 inch each way on flammable lines. No smell = no warning, so check with detectors. Butyl gloves + safety glasses = always.
t1-safe.examWarning: R-600a is approved for NEW household refrigerators only, NEVER for retrofitting existing units designed for other refrigerants. HC and HFO refrigerants have no odorants.

t2-leak.title: Leak Detection & Rates
t2-leak.lesson: Imagine a commercial rooftop unit slowly losing charge. How do you find the leak, and when does the law require action? First, the detection. The unit nameplate is the easiest way to check the refrigerant type and total charge. An electronic or ultrasonic leak detector is most effective for finding the general area of small leaks. Once the general area is found, soap bubbles pinpoint the exact location. For newly installed split systems, pressurize with dry nitrogen for leak testing. If using an electronic detector, add a trace of the system's design refrigerant to the nitrogen. Warning: using the wrong type of electronic detector with a flammable refrigerant can cause an explosion. Look for oil traces on operating hermetic systems known to be leaking -- oil on a shaft seal means a leak. Now the legal thresholds, effective January 1, 2019, for systems with 50 or more pounds of regulated refrigerant: comfort cooling = 10% per year, commercial refrigeration = 20% per year, industrial process refrigeration (IPR) = 30% per year. For dual-use appliances, the highest percentage of use determines the applicable rate.
t2-leak.memoryTrick: 10-20-30 rule: comfort=10, commercial=20, IPR=30. Electronic detector finds the neighborhood, soap bubbles find the house. Oil traces = leak clue.
t2-leak.examWarning: Dual-use appliances use the HIGHEST percentage of use to determine leak rate category. If 55% manufacturing and 45% AC, it is classified as IPR (30% rate).

t2-repair.title: Leak Repair & Compliance
t2-repair.lesson: Picture this: you just calculated that a 500-pound commercial refrigeration system has exceeded its 20% annual leak rate. The clock starts ticking. Once the threshold is exceeded on any system with 50 or more pounds of regulated refrigerant, the owner has 30 days to repair the appliance and conduct an initial verification leak test. If the system has 200 or more pounds and passes that initial test, a follow-up verification test is also required. If the owner cannot repair the leak to bring it below the threshold, the appliance must be retrofitted or retired. Not having a certified service technician available is NOT a valid reason to extend the deadline. Leak inspection schedules depend on system size: commercial refrigeration and IPR systems with more than 500 pounds require inspections every 3 months. Systems with 50 to 500 pounds require annual inspections. Comfort cooling with 50 or more pounds requires annual inspections. All visible and accessible components must be inspected. After a leak repair, always install a new filter drier and complete a standing-pressure leak test before recharging. The owner/operator must keep records of all inspections and tests for 3 years. The owner has 18 months to retrofit or retire if switching to an exempt refrigerant.
keyNumbers: 30 days to repair after exceeding threshold
18 months to retrofit/retire with exempt refrigerant
t2-repair.memoryTrick: 30 days to fix. 200+ lbs = follow-up. 500+ lbs = every 3 months. 3 years of records. Technician unavailability = NO excuse.
t2-repair.examWarning: After leak repair, ALWAYS install a new filter drier. The repair deadline cannot be extended because a technician is unavailable. Cold storage can operate up to 12 months but must report to EPA if leaking exceeds 125% of full charge in a calendar year.

t2-rec.title: High-Pressure Recovery
t2-rec.lesson: Imagine you are recovering refrigerant from a large commercial system. Type II technicians must own active recovery equipment. If the total charge is greater than 15 pounds, passive recovery cannot be used. Before starting, determine the total charge and check valve positions and oil level. If switching refrigerant types from your last recovery, remove all previous refrigerant from the recovery unit and change the filter. To reduce recovery time, connect to the liquid line first and recover in liquid phase. After liquid removal, switch to vapor recovery. Vapor recovery is slower but minimizes oil loss from the appliance. Cool the recovery cylinder and heat the system to increase the pressure difference and speed things up. Required evacuation levels for equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993: very high-pressure and high-pressure under 200 lbs = 0 inches Hg. High-pressure 200 lbs or more = 10 inches Hg. Medium-pressure under 200 lbs = 10 inches Hg. Medium-pressure 200 lbs or more = 15 inches Hg. After reaching vacuum, wait for pressure rise to check for moisture or leaks. If leaks prevent reaching the target level, recover to 0 psig. Hermetic recovery compressor motors rely on refrigerant flow for cooling and can overheat in deep vacuum.
keyNumbers: Very high + high <200 lbs = 0 in Hg
t2-rec.memoryTrick: Evacuation pattern: 0-10-10-15 (very high/high<200, high200+, med<200, med200+). Liquid first, then vapor. Cool tank + heat system = fast. 15 lbs = passive limit.
t2-rec.examWarning: Do not confuse evacuation levels. R-410A (high-pressure) with 200+ lbs = 10 in Hg. R-404A with 40 lbs (high-pressure, under 200) = 0 in Hg. If you cannot reach target, go to 0 psig.

t2-tech.title: Type II Charging & Diagnosis
t2-tech.lesson: Imagine you have just replaced the compressor on a large chilled-water system and need to charge it. For an R-410A chilled-water system, charge vapor from vacuum to approximately 110 psig, which corresponds to 36 degrees F, to prevent water from freezing in the tubes. Only after reaching that pressure do you add liquid. For an R-134a chiller, charge vapor to approximately 30 psig, corresponding to 35 degrees F. Key components to understand: the receiver sits in the liquid line at the condenser outlet in TXV systems, holding high-pressure liquid. Before opening a system with a receiver, pump down and isolate refrigerant there. The moisture-indicating sight glass is in the liquid line after the filter drier. Replace the filter drier anytime the system is opened. The accumulator sits between the evaporator and compressor, preventing liquid from entering the compressor. Suction and discharge valves are normally back-seated. Never close the discharge service valve on an operating reciprocating compressor -- doing so can cause damage and severe injury. Crankcase heaters reduce refrigerant dissolved in oil and prevent foaming at startup. If you smell a pungent odor during recovery, suspect burnout and take an oil sample to test for acid.
keyNumbers: R-410A: vapor to 110 psig (36F) before liquid
R-134a: vapor to 30 psig (35F) before liquid
t2-tech.memoryTrick: Vapor FIRST into evacuated chillers. 410A to 110 (easy to remember: 410=110). 134a to 30. Never close discharge on running compressor. Pungent = burnout.
t2-tech.examWarning: Charging liquid first into an evacuated chiller can freeze water in tubes. Always charge vapor first to raise pressure above freezing temperature. The receiver is at the condenser OUTLET, not inlet.

t2-ref.title: High-Pressure Refrigerant Properties
t2-ref.lesson: Think of refrigerants as having distinct fingerprints. For Type II work with medium, high, and very high-pressure systems, you need to know each one's identity. HFCs are the workhorses: R-134a is medium-pressure, A1 safety class, GWP of 1430, commonly used in automotive and commercial applications. R-410A is high-pressure, A1, GWP of 2090, the standard for modern air conditioning. R-404A is high-pressure, A1, GWP of 3920, used in low-temperature commercial refrigeration. R-407C is high-pressure, A1, GWP of 1770, common as an R-22 retrofit option. R-407C has high temperature glide and may easily experience fractionation. For lubricants: HCFC systems use alkylbenzene oil. HFC systems use polyolester (POE) oil. POE oil is very hygroscopic, absorbing moisture from air and through some containers. POE oil CANNOT be mixed with any other type of oil. Before retrofitting from HCFC to HFC, completely remove the old oil first. When checking for contamination, compare pressure and temperature to the PT chart. Both measurements must be stable. R-134a at 80F with no impurities reads approximately 87 psig. If the PT relationship does not match, the refrigerant may be contaminated and should go to a reclamation facility.
t2-ref.memoryTrick: HCFC = alkylbenzene. HFC = POE. POE is a sponge for moisture. Never mix POE with anything. R-407C = the fractionation poster child.
t2-ref.examWarning: Even though R-407C and R-410A are both high-pressure HFCs, R-407C is zeotropic (uses bubble/dew points) while R-410A uses single saturation values. They are NOT interchangeable.

t3-leak.title: Low-Pressure Leak Detection
t3-leak.lesson: Imagine a chiller operating in a vacuum. Unlike high-pressure systems where refrigerant leaks OUT, low-pressure systems leak AIR and MOISTURE IN. This fundamental difference changes everything about leak detection. Because these systems operate below atmospheric pressure, purge units are needed to remove non-condensables that sneak in. The purge unit draws from the top of the condenser where non-condensables accumulate and returns separated refrigerant to the evaporator. If the purge unit runs excessively, the system is leaking. High head pressure means air is in the system and the purge may not be working properly. For leak testing a low-pressure centrifugal chiller with nitrogen, the maximum test pressure is 10 psig. Exceeding 10 psig could cause the rupture disc to fail. For charged systems, the most efficient leak check uses heat from circulated hot water or heating blankets to create positive pressure. If excessive moisture continuously collects in the purge unit, suspect a tube leak in the condenser or chiller barrel. To check for water-side leaks, place a detector probe in the water box drain valve opening with water removed. Open-drive compressors are particularly susceptible to shaft seal leaks.
keyNumbers: 10 psig maximum nitrogen for low-pressure chillers
Purge draws from TOP of condenser
t3-leak.memoryTrick: Low pressure = air leaks IN (opposite of high pressure). 10 psig MAX nitrogen or you blow the rupture disc. Purge unit: TOP of condenser, back to evaporator. Excessive purging = leaky system.
t3-leak.examWarning: Never exceed 10 psig nitrogen when testing low-pressure systems. The rupture disc will fail. For charged systems, hot water is the best way to create positive pressure for leak checking.

t3-repair.title: Centrifugal Chiller Repair
t3-repair.lesson: Think of a centrifugal chiller as a massive, precision machine where repairs follow strict protocols. The same leak rate thresholds apply as Type II: comfort cooling = 10%, commercial = 20%, IPR = 30%. Most chillers are comfort cooling, so the 10% rate typically applies. If an appliance with 50 or more pounds exceeds its threshold, the owner has 30 days to repair. For a chiller with 200 pounds of ozone-depleting refrigerant that passes the initial verification test, a follow-up test is required. Mothballing means shutting down the system in place, not physically removing it. The owner has 18 months to retrofit or retire if switching to an exempt refrigerant. Technician unavailability is not a valid extension. Major repairs include replacing the compressor, condenser, or evaporator. For non-major repairs, warm the liquid refrigerant with hot water or warming blankets to bring system pressure out of vacuum to positive pressure. To maintain an idled low-pressure system, keep pressure slightly above atmospheric to prevent air entry. Moisture most frequently enters through leaks in gaskets or fittings. Drain the water sides of the evaporator and condenser before recovering refrigerant from a chiller suspected of having tube leaks. Records must be kept for 3 years.
t3-repair.memoryTrick: Chillers = comfort = 10%. Mothball = park it, do not move it. Hot water brings vacuum systems to positive pressure. Drain water BEFORE recovery from tube leaks.
t3-repair.examWarning: Mothballing does NOT require removing the appliance or storing it in a warehouse. It simply means shutting the system down in place. Idled systems must be kept slightly above atmospheric pressure.

t3-rec.title: Low-Pressure Recovery
t3-rec.lesson: Imagine recovering refrigerant from a 350-ton R-123 chiller. Even at 0 psig after removing all liquid, approximately 100 pounds of vapor refrigerant remains. That is a lot of refrigerant still in the system. Start with liquid removal, then switch to vapor recovery. The high-pressure cutout on the recovery unit is typically set at 10 psig. The rupture disc on a recovery vessel for low-pressure refrigerants relieves at 15 psig. During recovery, run the water pumps, recovery compressor, and condenser water. Circulate water through the chiller to prevent freezing. Raising the temperature speeds up recovery. When removing oil, heat it to 130 degrees F because less refrigerant stays trapped in hotter oil. A water-cooled recovery unit allows faster recovery of large quantities. If an oversized vacuum pump is used, it can freeze trapped water. Break vacuum with nitrogen to prevent freezing and speed dehydration. For heavy moisture, use triple evacuation with dry nitrogen. For disposal of low-pressure appliances, recover to 25 mm Hg absolute. After reaching the required vacuum, wait for pressure rise before proceeding. If leaks make the prescribed level unattainable, do not proceed with recovery. All equipment must be labeled as certified.
keyNumbers: 100 lbs vapor in 350-ton chiller at 0 psig
15 psig = rupture disc on recovery vessel
t3-rec.memoryTrick: Liquid first, vapor second. 10 psig cutout, 15 psig rupture disc. 130F for oil. 25 mm Hg for disposal. Circulate water or things freeze. 100 lbs of vapor hides in a 350-ton chiller.
t3-rec.examWarning: Do not proceed with recovery if you cannot reach the prescribed vacuum level. A 350-ton chiller at 0 psig still holds about 100 pounds of vapor -- do not assume the system is empty.

t3-rech.title: Low-Pressure Recharging
t3-rech.lesson: Imagine you have just evacuated and dehydrated a chilled-water system. You are eager to add refrigerant, but adding liquid first could freeze the water in the heat exchanger tubes. Here is why: after evacuation, the system is in deep vacuum. If you introduce liquid refrigerant at that vacuum, it will flash to very cold temperatures. For R-245fa, liquid charged at greater than 18 inches Hg vacuum will cause water to freeze because its saturation temperature at that point drops well below negative 40 degrees F. The correct procedure: reintroduce vapor first to raise the system to a saturation temperature above 32 degrees F (freezing point). For an R-123 chiller, bring vapor pressure above 20 inches Hg vacuum before adding liquid. Once at safe pressure, charge liquid through the evaporator charging valve, which is the lowest access point on a low-pressure appliance. Do not over-pressurize the system. Major repairs include replacing the compressor, condenser, or evaporator. For non-major repairs, warm liquid refrigerant with hot water or warming blankets to create positive pressure. A useful conversion: psig equals psia minus 14.7, and 14.7 psia equals 0 psig (atmospheric pressure). Use isopropyl alcohol to remove ice from sight glasses.
keyNumbers: 32F = minimum saturation temp before adding liquid
18 in Hg vacuum = R-245fa freeze danger point
20 in Hg vacuum = R-123 minimum before liquid
t3-rech.memoryTrick: Vapor FIRST, then liquid. 32F is the freeze line -- stay above it. R-123: above 20 in Hg vacuum. R-245fa: above 18 in Hg vacuum. Liquid enters at the LOW point (evaporator valve).
t3-rech.examWarning: Charging liquid R-245fa into a vacuum greater than 18 inches Hg will freeze water. For R-123, vapor pressure must be above 20 inches Hg vacuum before liquid charging.

t3-ref.title: Low-Pressure Refrigerant Properties
t3-ref.lesson: Think of low-pressure refrigerants as the gentle giants of the refrigerant world. They operate at 30 psig or lower at 104 degrees F, which means during normal operation they are actually in a vacuum. There are four low-pressure refrigerants to know. R-11 is a CFC with ODP of 1, GWP of 4657, ASHRAE class A1, used in air-conditioning chillers but now phased out. R-123 is an HCFC with ODP of 0.012, GWP of 77, classified B1 (higher toxicity but non-flammable), used in commercial chillers. At 35 degrees F, R-123 sits at 19.5 inches Hg vacuum. R-245fa is an HFC with GWP of 1030, classified B1 (also higher toxicity), used in low-pressure applications. R-1233zd is an HFO with GWP of just 1, classified A1 (non-flammable, lower toxicity), used in new chillers and foam blowing. Notice that R-1233zd is the standout: it is the only low-pressure HFO, has the lowest GWP of 1, and unlike most HFOs it is A1 (non-flammable). ASHRAE Standard 15 requires sensors to detect refrigerant in equipment rooms. Exception: R-717 (ammonia) rooms do not need a detector if mechanical ventilation provides continuous airflow. Room sensors are needed because refrigerants are heavier than air and displace oxygen.
keyNumbers: R-11: ODP=1, GWP=4657, A1, CFC, phased out
t3-ref.memoryTrick: Four low-pressure refrigerants: 11, 123, 245fa, 1233zd. Two are B1 (toxic): R-123 and R-245fa. R-1233zd is the star: GWP=1, A1, non-flammable. R-123 at 35F = 19.5 vacuum.
t3-ref.examWarning: Do not confuse R-1233zd (A1, non-flammable) with other HFOs that are A2L. R-123 is B1 (higher toxicity). Both R-123 and R-245fa are B1 despite being different refrigerant types (HCFC vs HFC).
